mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XJ 2.7d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned AJD-V6 2.7-litre engine typically costs £4,000–£6,500 supply only. Used engines are available from £2,500–£4,500. Most Jaguar engine replacements range from £3,000 to £9,000 depending on the model and engine type. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines are no longer available.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar XJ 2.7d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 2.7d is a capable and powerful engine with a very good reputation for long-term reliability. However, it has known weak points: big end bearing wear (premature wear can cause crankshaft seizure), turbocharger failure (oil leaks can cause bent con rods and scored liners), EGR valve clogging (two valves, one per bank), DPF clogging, and timing chain stretch/tensioner wear. Regular oil changes every 10,000 miles are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XJ 2.7d?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are big end bearing wear (metallic knocking, crankshaft seizure), turbocharger failure (oil leaks, bent con rods, scored liners), EGR valve clogging, DPF blockage, timing chain stretch/tensioner wear, and injector issues. The engine also has a timing chain, not a belt, which can stretch over time.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar XJ 2.7d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £8,000 XJ 2.7d, a £5,000 recon is 63% — approaching our 60% Jaguar threshold. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon exceeds the threshold — get a scrap valuation first. Well-maintained XJ 2.7ds are becoming rarer — a proper restoration is often justified for a clean example.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar XJ 2.7d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The XJ 2.7d carries the engine code AJD-V6 2.7 (also referred to as 7G in some documentation). It's a 2.7-litre twin-turbo V6 diesel engine used in the XJ X350/X358 from 2005 to 2009. The badge on the tailgate is the easiest tell — '2.7d' or 'TDVi'.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the big end bearings, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Expert engineers can grind the crankshaft to remove imperfections and install oversized bearings. However, this requires timely intervention before irreversible damage occurs. If the crankshaft snaps or the engine seizes, a full engine replacement (£4,000–£7,500) is typically required. Regular oil changes (10,000-mile intervals) are the single most effective preventative measure.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the 2.7-litre AJD-V6 diesel engine. Most Jaguar engine replacements range from £3,000 to £9,000 depending on the model and engine type. Reconditioned engines are available in the £4,000–£6,500 range. A used 2.7d engine typically costs £3,000–£6,000 depending on condition and mileage. Main dealer new engines are no longer available.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an XJ 2.7d engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The aluminium-bodied XJ's engine bay is tight but accessible.

problems.title: Big End Bearing Wear & Crankshaft Seizure
problems.symptoms: Metallic knocking from the bottom end, especially under load, low oil pressure warning, engine seizure, catastrophic failure.
problems.repairCost: £2,000–£4,000 (crankshaft grind and oversized bearings)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,500 (if crankshaft snaps or block is damaged)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear knocking, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: Big end bearing wear is one of the most concerning faults reported on the 2.7d engine. When bearings wear excessively, they can stick to the crankshaft, leading to engine seizure. In severe cases, the crankshaft can snap entirely, destroying the engine block. Expert engineers can grind the crankshaft and install oversized bearings if caught early. Regular oil changes at 10,000-mile intervals are essential. If you hear knocking from the bottom end, do not drive the car — catastrophic engine failure is imminent.

failureLink.label: Read the full engine bearing failure guide

problems.title: Turbocharger Failure & Bent Connecting Rods
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, excessive smoke, oil consumption, metallic tapping from the engine, poor acceleration.
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,500 (if bent con rods and scored liners require engine rebuild)
problems.recommendation: When a turbocharger begins to leak oil, that oil can be drawn into the engine's inlet system. Under certain conditions, a surge of hot oil can enter the engine, causing connecting rods to bend. A bent connecting rod can force the piston to press against the cylinder walls at an angle, scoring the liners and destroying the cylinder seal. This can write off the engine. Early detection is essential — if you notice excessive smoke or loss of power, have the turbocharger inspected immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbocharger failure guide

problems.title: EGR Valve Clogging & DPF Blockage
problems.symptoms: Restricted performance warning, poor acceleration, engine management light, failed regeneration cycles, oil contamination.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (EGR valve replacement — two valves)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: The 2.7d has two EGR valves, one per bank of the V6 engine. Both are prone to clogging with soot, especially on urban-driven vehicles. The DPF is also sensitive to short journeys — the regeneration cycle can fail if the car is not driven on long enough runs. A few longer journeys every couple of weeks usually keeps the DPF in good order. If you experience restricted performance warnings or poor acceleration, have the EGR valves and DPF inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full EGR & DPF failure guide

rows.whenItMakesSense: If caught early, crankshaft can be ground and oversized bearings fitted
Early detection saves the engine; replace turbo before oil contamination causes bent rods
Replacement or cleaning — cheaper than engine failure
Forced regeneration or DPF clean — less than replacement

buyingChecks.title: Buying This XJ 2.7d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Jaguar service history with 10,000-mile oil changes — the factory 16,000-mile interval is too long for this engine
* No bottom-end knocking or metallic sounds
* EGR valves cleaned or replaced within the last 40,000 miles
avoidIf: * Metallic knocking from the bottom end (big end bearing failure warning)
* Poor service history — this engine is unforgiving of neglect
* Restricted performance warnings with no diagnosis
* Evidence of DPF or EGR faults with no documented repair

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The XJ 2.7d is a refined and powerful diesel option for the aluminium-bodied XJ, offering excellent fuel economy and low-down torque. The engine itself has a very good reputation for long-term reliability, but it has known weak points: big end bearing wear, turbocharger failure, EGR clogging, and DPF issues. These are well-understood and manageable with diligent maintenance. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained XJ 2.7d is a £5,000–£12,000 future classic; a neglected one is a £5,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare XJ 2.7d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar XJ 2.7d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for knocking, tapping, or rattling (big end bearing or timing chain warning)
* Service History — including the 2.7d era (2005–2009) and confirmed oil-change intervals — ideally every 10,000 miles
* Turbo Check — Any history of turbocharger work? (oil leaks can cause catastrophic engine damage)
* EGR Check — Have the EGR valves been cleaned or replaced? (two valves, one per bank)
* DPF Check — Evidence of regular motorway driving? (urban driving clogs the DPF)
* Oil Level — Check the dipstick; any evidence of oil contamination or diesel dilution?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved turbo, EGR, DPF, or bearing-related codes?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
